sections.title: Overview
sections.content: This guideline covers screening of recreational sports and gym injuries common on remote sites, including lifting, running, contact, and court or field activities.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Pain or injury after gym, sport, or recreational activity
- Sudden pop, strain, twist, or overload during training
- Swelling, bruising, instability, or reduced performance
- Fear of returning to training or re-injury

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Recreational injuries can still be fractures, tendon ruptures, concussion, or dislocation patterns
- Patients may minimise injury because they want to return to exercise quickly
- Gym injuries often reflect rapid load progression, fatigue, or poor movement control rather than one catastrophic event

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Screen trauma severity, neurovascular status, concussion red flags, and whether the patient can bear weight or use the limb safely
- Restrict return to play or training if function is clearly compromised
- Escalate if fracture, rupture, dislocation, or concussion concern exists

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Clarify the exact mechanism, load, movement pattern, and previous injury history
- Use staged rehab and return progression rather than symptom-only rest or immediate unrestricted return
- Coordinate imaging or medical review when the mechanism or dysfunction suggests significant structural injury

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - On remote sites, the safest sports plan is one that prevents a minor recreation injury turning into a work limitation
